title: Who Is the Greatest Basketball Player of All Time?
prompt: Determine who deserves the title of greatest basketball player of all time. Consider statistical dominance, championship success, era-adjusted performance, impact on winning, individual awards, longevity, cultural influence on the game, and how different definitions of 'greatest' produce different answers.

agents.bio: Retired NBA scout who worked for the Bulls during the second three-peat. Believes the 6-0 Finals record, two three-peats, five MVPs, ten scoring titles, and the greatest individual peak in league history closes the debate. Dismisses longevity arguments as participation trophies. Thinks advanced metrics favoring other players are retrofitting data to predetermined conclusions.

Analytics writer covering the NBA since 2003 who watched LeBron's entire career unfold. Believes his all-time scoring record, four titles with three franchises, 20 years of elite production, and dominance across every statistical category makes him the most complete player ever. Argues carrying the 2007 and 2018 Cavs to the Finals outweighs anything Jordan did alongside Pippen.

Basketball history professor and author of three books on the evolution of the NBA. Specializes in pre-merger basketball and argues the GOAT conversation is incomplete without Russell's 11 rings in 13 seasons and Kareem's six MVPs with the most unstoppable shot ever. Has watched thousands of hours of archived film. Skeptical any single player can be ranked across fundamentally different eras.

Former MIT Sloan Analytics presenter who builds era-adjusted player impact models. Published peer-reviewed work on basketball wins above replacement. Her models place Jordan's peak and LeBron's career value at the top but in different dimensions. Believes the debate is really about whether you value the highest summit or the longest mountain range.

Sports columnist and former player agent who represented several All-Stars. Believes the GOAT debate is about narrative and cultural impact, not statistics. Jordan's influence on globalizing basketball and competitive mythology transcends box scores. Also argues Kobe Bryant belongs in the top-tier conversation that analytics people exclude because his game was aesthetically dominant but statistically inefficient.
